questions.question: What makes resharding riskier than ordinary rebalancing?
options: The shard map or key shape itself changes, so compatibility and client behavior may need to change too.
Rebalancing always copies more bytes than resharding.
Resharding never requires any validation or rollback.
The old layout can never coexist with a new one.
questions.explanation: Resharding changes layout semantics, not just physical location.

questions.question: Why are migration cohorts useful during resharding?
options: They limit blast radius and allow gradual cutover, validation, and rollback.
They guarantee dual-write logic is unnecessary.
They eliminate the need for observability during migration.
They make every background job automatically idempotent.
questions.explanation: Cohorts are a practical control for safe incremental migration.

questions.question: What is the safest high-level order for a live resharding plan?
options: Prepare compatibility, backfill, keep up with changes, validate parity, then cut over gradually.
Cut over all traffic immediately and backfill only if customers complain.
Delete the old shard map before new clients are deployed.
Avoid any temporary coexistence because it complicates the diagram.
questions.explanation: Compatibility and phased validation are what make live resharding survivable.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of dual-layout compatibility?
options: It greatly improves migration safety, but increases temporary complexity in routing and writes.
It removes the need for parity checks.
It guarantees no extra storage cost during migration.
It ensures every client upgrades instantly.
questions.explanation: Supporting old and new layouts together costs complexity, but buys safety.

questions.question: Which metric is most important before final cohort cutover?
options: Parity mismatch rate and version adoption between old and new layouts.
The length of the migration channel name in chat.
The number of TODO comments in source files.
The total count of build pipelines in the company.
questions.explanation: You need evidence the new layout is correct and actually being used safely.

questions.question: Why do secondary indexes deserve explicit mention in a resharding answer?
options: They may depend on the old key or layout and can be incorrect even if primary backfill appears complete.
They always migrate automatically with no special handling.
They are only relevant for analytics systems, not serving paths.
They make rollback impossible by definition.
questions.explanation: Derived structures can lag or diverge and must be validated directly.

questions.question: What is the real danger of a big-bang shard-map switch?
options: A single compatibility or correctness mistake can impact the whole system with little rollback granularity.
It always uses less bandwidth than a phased approach.
It guarantees lower complexity for clients and operators.
It makes version skew impossible.

questions.question: When might a parity mismatch threshold still be acceptable for cutover?
options: When the mismatches are understood, bounded, non-user-visible, and explicitly accepted with a cleanup plan.
Whenever the team is tired of waiting on validation.
Whenever the new shard count is larger than the old one.
Only when observability is disabled and no one can measure the difference.
questions.explanation: The right answer is nuanced: some mismatches may be acceptable, but only if understood and controlled.
